text.title: direnv + mise: Examples
text.summary: NERO multi-version Python setup (3.12 for most repos, 3.11 for one), full .envrc workflow, troubleshooting wrong Python version.

example.code:
# Install both versions once mise install python@3.11.11 mise install python@3.12.8
# Set global fallback mise use --global python@3.12.8
# Per-project override cd ~/workspace/repos/nero-core mise use python@3.12.8 # creates .tool-versions
cd ~/projects/meetingtax/be mise use python@3.11.11 # creates .tool-versions
# Verify cd ~/workspace/repos/nero-core && mise current # python 3.12.8 /path/to/.tool-versions
cd ~/projects/meetingtax/be && mise current # python 3.11.11 /path/to/.tool-versions

section.title: First-Time Setup for a New Project

example.code:
cd ~/workspace/repos/new-project
# Set Python version mise use python@3.12.8
# Create .envrc cat > .envrc << 'EOF' use mise layout python-venv .venv dotenv_if_exists .env watch_file requirements.txt EOF
# Trust the .envrc (required after create or edit) direnv allow
# direnv loads: Python 3.12.8, .venv activated python --version # Python 3.12.8 which python # ~/workspace/repos/new-project/.venv/bin/python
# Install deps pip install -r requirements.txt

# Diagnose python --version # shows unexpected version mise current # shows which version mise thinks is active direnv status # shows if .envrc is loaded and allowed
# Common fixes direnv allow # .envrc wasn't allowed direnv reload # .envrc changed but not reloaded mise use python@3.12.8 # .tool-versions missing
# Check .bashrc hook order grep -E "mise|direnv" ~/.bashrc # Correct: mise before direnv
